name: Industrial Robots
how: Servo motion and vision automate repetitive tasks.

name: General-Purpose Humanoids
what: Bipedal robots for varied physical tasks.

name: Service & Logistics Robots
what: Autonomous robots for warehouses and delivery.
how: Navigation and grasping handle goods movement.

name: Humanoid Robots in Manufacturing
what: Integration of AI and robotics for flexible assembly lines.
how: Development of dexterous robotic arms and collaborative robots.

name: Advanced Humanoid Service Robots
what: Develops human-like robots with advanced AI and dexterity for various service roles.
how: Uses AI for natural language processing, facial recognition, and gesture control.

name: Humanoid Robots for Service Industries
what: Humanoid robots can perform tasks that require dexterity and human-like interactions.
how: These robots can be used in retail, hospitality, and healthcare settings to provide personalized services.

name: Humanoid Service Robots
what: Develops humanoid robots for various service applications.
how: Combines AI and robotics to provide assistance in healthcare and other fields.

name: Robot Foundation Models
what: General-purpose AI models that map what a robot sees and is told into the actions it should take, across many tasks and robot bodies.
how: Vision-language-action models are trained on large mixed datasets of robot demonstrations so skills transfer between tasks and hardware.

name: Artificial-Muscle Actuators
what: Soft, muscle-like actuators that contract on command, offering quieter and more compliant motion than motors and gears.
how: Electrohydraulic (HASEL) or polymer actuators change shape under voltage, converting electrical input into muscle-like pulling force.
use: Soft robotics, safe human-robot interaction research

name: Humanoid Robots in Factories
what: Early on-site trials of general-purpose humanoid robots doing real material-handling tasks alongside workers.
how: Bipedal robots with grippers are trained on target tasks and deployed in supervised pilots to move totes, load lines and sort items.

name: Dexterous Robotic Hands
what: Multi-fingered robot hands aiming to match human-like grasping and in-hand manipulation.
how: Tendon-driven fingers with tactile sensing are paired with learning-based control so the hand can reorient and handle varied objects.

name: Autonomous Mobile Manipulation
what: Robots that combine a mobile base with an arm to pick and move diverse items in unstructured warehouses.
how: Learned perception identifies and grasps novel objects while navigation moves the robot between stations, adapting to changing inventory.
